coredata.dc:title: Upconversion nanoparticles/carbon dots (UCNPs@CDs) composite for simultaneous detection and speciation of divalent and trivalent iron ions
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Yi-Lin
Zhang, Xiao-Ping
Zhao, Chen-Xi
Liu, Xiangju
Shu, Yang
Wang, Jian-Hua
Liu, Nazhen
coredata.dc:description: In this study, the application of carbon dots (CDs) modified NaYF4:Yb, Er nanoparticles (UCNPs@CDs) as the fluorescent nanoprobe for simultaneous detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ was investigated. Fe3+ quantification (5–80 μmol L−1) was achieved, as a result of Fe3+ induced fluorescence quenching of UCNPs@CDs at 434 nm (under the 336 nm excitation). The chelate (Fe2+-phen) formed by Fe2+ and 1,10-phenanthroline had a broad absorption centered at 510 nm, due to inner filter effect (IFE), Fe2+ quantification (4–120 μmol L−1) was achieved as a result of (Fe2+-phen) induced fluorescence quenching of UCNPs@CDs at 545 nm (under the 980 nm excitation). The resultant UCNPs@CDs probe, with excellent anti-interference capability, favorable fluorescence stability, and convincing performance in real sample analysis, showed promising application in simultaneous detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Simultaneous detection
Speciation
Fe2+
Fe3+
Composite nanomaterials

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of NaYF4:Yb, Er upconversion nanoparticles
Determination of Fe2+ and Fe3+ in real samples
Preparation and characterization of the UCNPs@CDs
Responsiveness of the UCNPs@CDs to Fe2+ and Fe3+
Simultaneous detection and speciation of Fe2+ and Fe3+ in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21974018 , 21727811 , and 22074011 ), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( N2005015 , and N2005027 ), and Liaoning Revitalization Talents Program ( XLYC1907191 , XLYC1802016 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM image and (B) TEM image of OA-UCNPs. (C) TEM image and (D) high-resolution TEM image of UCNPs@CDs. The insets in B and D showed the corresponding photographs of powders.

(A) FT-IR spectra of OA-UCNPs and UCNPs@CDs, (B) UV/vis absorption spectra of CDs, OA-UCNPs and UCNPs@CDs, (C) Wide scan XPS spectra and (D) High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s of OA-UCNPs and UCNPs@CDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of UCNPs@CDs (under the 980 nm excitation) with different concentrations of Fe2+. (B) The corresponding curve graph between the ratio-fluorescence of I545 nm/I660 nm and Fe2+ concentrations.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of UCNPs@CDs (under the 336 nm excitation) with different concentrations of Fe3+. (B) The corresponding curve graph between the ratio-fluorescence of I434 nm/I0 434nm and Fe3+ concentrations.

The variation of fluorescence intensity of UCNPs@CDs. (A, B) The concentrations of metal cations and amino acids were 20 μmol L−1, λex = 980 nm. (C, D) The concentrations of metal cations and amino acids were 100 μmol L−1, λex = 336 nm. Inserts: the value of fluorescence intensity relative to blank.
The content of Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions in unsealed ferrous lactate oral solution samples after oxidation in air for 0–7 days.
Schematic of the UCNPs@CDs fluorescence sensing platform for simultaneous detection and speciation of Fe2+ and Fe3+.

head.ce:title: Upconversion nanoparticles/carbon dots (UCNPs@CDs) composite for simultaneous detection and speciation of divalent and trivalent iron ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang, 110819, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang, 110819, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Marine Environmental Corrosion and Bio-fouling, Institute of Oceanology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Qingdao, 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Marine Environmental Corrosion and Bio-fouling

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Marine Environmental Corrosion and Bio-fouling, Institute of Oceanology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Qingdao, 266071, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the application of carbon dots (CDs) modified NaYF4:Yb, Er nanoparticles (UCNPs@CDs) as the fluorescent nanoprobe for simultaneous detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ was investigated. Fe3+ quantification (5–80 μmol L−1) was achieved, as a result of Fe3+ induced fluorescence quenching of UCNPs@CDs at 434 nm (under the 336 nm excitation). The chelate (Fe2+-phen) formed by Fe2+ and 1,10-phenanthroline had a broad absorption centered at 510 nm, due to inner filter effect (IFE), Fe2+ quantification (4–120 μmol L−1) was achieved as a result of (Fe2+-phen) induced fluorescence quenching of UCNPs@CDs at 545 nm (under the 980 nm excitation). The resultant UCNPs@CDs probe, with excellent anti-interference capability, favorable fluorescence stability, and convincing performance in real sample analysis, showed promising application in simultaneous detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron is an essential element in cellular homeostasis and metabolism [1]. It is also one of the most indispensable elements in the growth and development of human body [2]. As a result, many physiological and pathological disorders could be reflected by the abnormal iron level in human body [3]. Excessive Fe3+ could cause serious diseases, including cancers, Parkinson, Alzheimer [4]; excessive Fe2+ in the living cell will induce the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) that may damage cell membranes, nucleic acids, proteins, and DNA [5,6]. Since Fe2+ is less stable and can be easily oxidized to Fe3+ by O2, the speciation and simultaneous detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ are challenging and necessary for clarifying the biological disorders related to iron.

The applied strategies for determination of iron include inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [7], high performance liquid chromatography [8], atomic spectrometry [9], capillary electrophoreses [10], and chemiluminescence methods [11]. However, tedious sample pretreatment procedures are required in most methods, and the simultaneous detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ can't be achieved.

Fluorescence-based strategies have been developed for discrimination of Fe2+ and Fe3+ over the past ten years [12,13]. Upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs), which can convert near-infrared light absorption into visible light emission, have attracted widespread attention. They have been considered as a promising probe for sensing and bioimaging, owing to several excellent advantages such as high tissue-penetration depth, negligible autofluorescence background, and low photobleaching [14–17]. In addition, UCNPs doped with different lanthanides were reported to possess multiple narrow emission peaks and large Stokes shifts [18–20], which could realize multi-channel detection. With low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility and optical stability [21], the application of carbon dots (CDs) in bioimaging, chemical sensing and photocatalytic reactions has been extensively studied. For example, kinds of CDs have been utilized as a biosensor for detecting metal ions [22–24]. By integrating UCNPs with CDs, composite UCNPs@CDs is expected to possess optical properties of both UCNPs and CDs.

In this work, UCNPs@CDs was prepared, and its application as a fluorescent nanoprobe for detection of Fe3+ and Fe2+ was investigated. As shown in Scheme 1 , Fe3+ can be quantified via fluorescence quenching of UCNPs@CDs at λex/λem 336/434 nm Fe2+ can be quantified via fluorescence quenching of UCNPs@CDs at λex/λem 980/545 nm. Furthermore, the performance of UCNPs@CDs for simultaneous detection of Fe3+ and Fe2+ in real samples was studied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NaYF4:Yb, Er upconversion nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: NaYF4:Yb, Er UCNPs were prepared according to a literature procedure [25,26]. Briefly, 0.78 mmol of YCl3·6H2O, 0.20 mmol of YbCl3·6H2O, and 0.02 mmol of ErCl3 were first mixed in a 100-mL beaker. After addition of ethanol (16.0 mL) and deionized water (16.0 mL) at room temperature, a homogeneous solution was obtained after 10 min of magnetic stirring. 16.0 mL of oleic acid (OA) was then added into the above solution, and the magnetic stirring was kept for another 30 min. Subsequently, 504.0 mg of NaF dissolved in 10.0 mL of ethanol and 10.0 mL of water was added dropwise to the above solution under stirring. The resultant solution was ultrasonically agitated for 30 min, and was transferred into a 150 mL Teflon-lined autoclave followed by a heat treatment at 200 °C for 7 h. The UCNPs were collected by centrifugation at 8500 rpm for 5 min, and the precipitation was washed three times with ethanol. Finally, the product was dried at 60 °C for 12 h.

The UCNPs@CDs were prepared using a modified procedure [27,28]. 1.0507 g of citric acid and 335 μL of ethylene diamine were first dissolved into 10 mL of water under magnetic stirring. Then, 50 mg of the as-prepared UCNPs dispersed in 3 mL of water were added and the above mixture was under magnetic stirring for another 10 min. Subsequently, the solution was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave followed by a heat treatment at 200 °C for 5 h. The UCNPs@CDs were collected by centrifugation at 8500 rpm for 5 min, and the precipitation was washed three times with ethanol and water. Finally, the brown product was dried at 60 °C for 12 h.

The detection of Fe2+ was performed in an MES buffer (4-morpholineethanesulfonic acid hydrate, pH 5.0, 10 mM) at room temperature. Typically, 60 μL of UCNPs@CDs (5 mg mL−1) was mixed with various amounts of Fe2+ to obtain final Fe2+ concentrations of 4–120 μmol L−14.5 μL phen (1,10-phenanthroline, 0.1 mol L−1) was then added into the solution and the mixture was diluted to 300 μL with MES buffer and shaken for 5 min before recording the fluorescence spectra at λex 980 nm.
The detection of Fe3+ was similar to the above procedure, except that phen was not required. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at λex 336 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Fe2+ and Fe3+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: 15 mg of Fe3O4 nanoparticles were dispersed in 3 mL of HCl (1 mol L−1). In order to prevent Fe2+ from being oxidized, the resultant Fe3O4 suspension (5 mg mL−1) was purged with N2. Subsequently, the degradation of nanoparticles was accelerated by 2 h of sonicate. The supernatant was collected after magnetic separation and then filtered by a 0.22-μm membrane. Finally, the pH of the filtered supernatant was adjusted to be 5.0 with NaOH solution. The subsequent detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ was performed according the procedures in section 2.3.

For the simultaneous detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ in ferrous lactate oral solution, the unsealed samples were immediately diluted 25-fold with MES buffer (pH 5.0) and ready for detection. In the contrastive experiments, unsealed ferrous lactate oral solutions were placed in the air for 2–7 days at room temperature and then diluted 25-fold with MES buffer (pH 5.0) before detection.

The human serum samples were obtained from Northeastern University Hospital. Serum samples were diluted 100-fold with MES buffer (pH 5.0) before analysis, and the diluted samples were added with different concentrations of standard Fe2+ and Fe3+ solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the UCNPs@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Scheme 1, the UCNPs@CDs were prepared by mixing oleic acid modified UCNPs (OA-UCNPs) with CDs precursors, and a subsequent facile solvothermal treatment.

The morphologies of OA-UCNPs and UCNPs@CDs were first checked with SEM and TEM (Fig. 1 A–C). It could be observed that the morphology of OA-UCNPs was irregular, as a result of harsh preparation conditions applied [29]. After modification, the color of OA-UCNPs changed from white to light brown, indicating a successful preparation of UCNPs@CDs. However, the TEM image of UCNPs@CDs showed no observed change in morphology after modification. The lattice spacing of 0.298 nm was observed on the HR-TEM image of UCNPs@CDs (Fig. 1D), which was consistent with the interplanar spacing of the (110) plane of hexagonal-phase NaYF4. However, the crystalline structure of CDs was not observed, indicting the attached CDs were amorphous [27,30].

OA-UCNPs and UCNPs@CDs were further characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. As shown in the spectrum of OA-UCNPs in Fig. 2 A, the absorptions mainly originate from the functional groups in the oleic group, e.g., the absorptions at 3425 and 1628 cm−1 were assigned to the vibrations of O–H and CO bond from the carboxyl group; the absorptions at 2925, 2858 and 1464 cm−1 were assigned to the vibrations of C–H bonds in the methylene group [31,32]. After formation of UCNPs@CDs, the intensities of peaks at 2925 and 2858 cm−1 decreased significantly, which was probably due to the modification of CDs on the surface of UCNPs, so that the peaks associated with the OA group were covered. This observation indicated that the UCNPs@CDs had been successfully prepared.

Fig. 2B showed the UV–vis spectra of CDs, OA-UCNPs and UCNPs@CDs. The characteristic absorption peak of CDs at 338 nm was observed on the spectrum of UCNPs@CDs, indicating the successful surface modification with CDs. The XRD analysis of OA-UCNPs and UCNPs@CDs (Figure S1B) suggested that the crystallographic structure of hexagonal phase NaYF4:Yb, Er was maintained during the modification process. The zeta potentials (Figure S1A) of the OA-UCNPs and UCNPs@CDs were −3.18 ± 0.35 mV and 0.29 ± 0.07 mV, respectively. The increase in zeta potential was due to the numerous –NH2 on the surface of the CDs, indicating the CDs were successfully modified on the surface of UCNPs.

The chemical composition on the surface of OA-UCNPs and UCNPs@CDs was investigated by XPS. The full scan spectra of OA-UCNPs and UCNPs@CDs (Fig. 2C) confirmed the presence of Y 3 d (161.2 eV), C 1s (286.5 eV), Y 3p (303.1 eV), N 1s (398.7 eV), O 1s (538.6 eV), F 1s (685.2 eV) and Na 1s (1073.5 eV) [28]. Deconvolution of the C 1s high resolution XPS spectrum of OA-UCNPs (Fig. 2D) showed three peaks at 284.3, 285.6 and 288.6 eV, which were associated with C–C/CC, C–O and CO, respectively. After coated with CDs, the C 1s spectra showed peaks at 284.5, 286.1 and 288.3 eV, which can be attributed to C–C/CC, C–N and CO/CN [33,34]. The presence of C–N and CN peaks in the spectrum of UCNPs@CDs confirmed the successful modification of OA-UCNPs with CDs.

As shown in Figure S2A, the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of CDs in composite UCNPs@CDs were 336 and 434 nm, respectively. Under 980 nm NIR excitation, the fluorescence (FL) intensity of the UCNPs@CDs decreased slightly compared to the OA-UCNPs (Figure S2B). An excellent fluorescence stability of UCNPs@CDs was confirmed under irradiation for 60 min (Figure S3). The FL intensity of UCNPs@CDs was found to be insensitive to NaCl concentrations in the range of 0.1–0.5 mol L−1 (Figure. S4). Figure S5 showed that while the FL intensity of UCNPs remained constant as the pH increased from 3 to 6.5, the FL intensity of CDs increased slightly. A lot of CDs have been reported to be highly sensitive to pH [35–37], and have been used as probes to monitor pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Responsiveness of the UCNPs@CDs to Fe2+ and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: First, the Fe2+ sensing mechanism of UCNPs@CDs was investigated. As shown in Figure S6A, the chelate Fe2+-phen had a broad absorption peak centered at 510 nm, and the absorption increased gradually with the increase of Fe2+ content. The absorption of Fe2+-phen obviously overlapped with the emission peak of UCNPs@CDs at 545 nm (under the 980 nm excitation) (Figure S6B), thus the fluorescence peak of the latter declined due to the inner filter effect (IFE) between Fe2+-phen and UCNPs@CDs. It was observed that the presence of Fe3+-phen would not cause the reduction of peak intensity of UCNPs@CDs at 545 nm. In addition, the fluorescence emission of UCNPs@CDs could be quenched only when both phen and Fe2+ were added (Figure S7). The ratio of Fe2+ to phen was optimized to be 1:5 (Figure S8A), and the detection could be finished within 3 min (Figure S8B).

Under the optimal conditions, the Fe2+ sensing performance of UCNPs@CDs was investigated. Fig. 3 A showed that the FL intensity of UCNPs@CDs at 545 nm (under the 980 nm excitation) decreased as the Fe2+ concentration increased in the range of 4–120 μmol L−1. The linear regression equation was expressed as I545 nm/I660 nm = −0.05599 C (Fe2+) + 28.6165 (R2 = 0.9958) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 3.17 μmol L−1 (Fig. 3B).

Subsequently, the Fe3+ sensing performance of UCNPs@CDs was investigated. As shown in Figure S9A, the FL intensity was stabilized within 5 min. The impact of different pH values on quenching was investigated. The results showed that the highest quenching efficiency was observed at pH 5.0 (Figure S9B). An acidic buffer solution (pH 5.0) was chosen for the detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ in this study [38]. Under the optimal conditions, the FL intensity at 434 nm was gradually decreased (under the 336 nm excitation) with the increment of Fe3+ concentrations (Fig. 4 A). The regression equation was I434 nm/I0 434 nm = −0.00626 C (Fe3+) + 0.858 (R2 = 0.9937, 5–80 μmol L−1) with a LOD of 1.53 μmol L−1 (Fig. 4B).

According to previous studies, an electron transfer mechanism was proposed for the quenching of the fluorescence emission of UCNPs@CDs by Fe3+, which had been widely used for Fe3+ detection [39–41]. As a result of the strong interaction between the hydroxyl and carboxylic acid groups of the CDs and Fe3+ ions [27], the excited electrons of UCNPs@CDs could be easily transferred to the half-filled d orbitals of Fe3+, leading to the fluorescence quenching of UCNPs@CDs. In addition, the fluorescence decay dynamics in Figure S10 suggested the addition of Fe3+ leaded to a decrease of fluorescence lifetime (τ) from 9.19 to 7.36 ns. The decrease of lifetime indicated a fast electron-transfer process between UCNPs@CDs and Fe3+.

To evaluate the anti-interference of this method and its application prospect in real sample analysis, the FL intensites of UCNPs@CDs in various coexisting metal cations and amino acids were recorded and summarized in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5A and B showed the quenching effect of Fe2+ on the FL at 545 nm, while the peak intensities remained unchanged (or slightly changed) in the presence of interferences under study. Fig. 5C and D showed the quenching effect of Fe3+ on the FL at 434 nm excited at 336 nm, while the peak intensities changed slightly in the presence of interferences. The above results indicated the UCNPs@CDs composite had excellent selectivity for the detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous detection and speciation of Fe2+ and Fe3+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The simultaneous detection and speciation of Fe2+/Fe3+ in Fe3O4 by the developed method was conducted. As shown in Table S1, the content of Fe3+ ions was ∼2.6 times of Fe2+ ions, indicating a faster release of Fe3+ during the decomposition of Fe3O4 in acid. The total Fe content obtained from the developed method was consistent with the ICP-MS result, confirming the credibility of the developed method.

The simultaneous detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ in unsealed ferrous lactate oral solution samples was conducted by the developed method either. Fig. 6 showed that the sealed sample contained only Fe2+. As the storage time in air increased, there was a gradual increase of Fe3+ concentration up to ∼3.8 mmol L−1 after 7 days of oxidation in air. In addition, UCNPs@CDs was applied to determinate Fe2+ and Fe3+ in human serum samples. As shown in Table S2, the obtained recoveries were in the range of 96.07–110.03%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) was less than 3.65%. The results indicated the successful application of the nanoprobe in real sample analysis.

In the present study, a novel approach for the simultaneous detection and speciation of Fe2+ and Fe3+ was developed. By combining the fluorescence response originated from UCNPs and CDs in composite UCNPs@CDs, the simultaneous detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ was accomplished. Fe3+ quantification (5–80 μmol L−1) was achieved, as a result of Fe3+ induced fluorescence quenching of UCNPs@CDs at 434 nm (under the 336 nm excitation). The chelate (Fe2+-phen) formed by Fe2+ and 1,10-phenanthroline had a broad absorption centered at 510 nm, due to inner filter effect, Fe2+ quantification (4–120 μmol L−1) was achieved as a result of (Fe2+-phen) induced fluorescence quenching of UCNPs@CDs at 545 nm (under the 980 nm excitation). The resultant probe was shown to have excellent anti-interference capability, favorable fluorescence stability in high ionic strength medium, and convincing performance in real sample analysis.

Yi-Lin Sun: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Xiao-Ping Zhang: Resources, Data curation. Chen-Xi Zhao: Data curation. Xiangju Liu: Resources. Yang Shu: Supervision, Project administration. Jian-Hua Wang: Supervision. Nazhen Liu: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21974018, 21727811, and 22074011), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N2005015, and N2005027), and Liaoning Revitalization Talents Program (XLYC1907191, XLYC1802016).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2021.338973.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Iron uptake and metabolism in the new millennium

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Halliwell, Reactive oxygen species and the central nervous system, J. Neurochem. 59 (1992) 1609-1621.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Iron deficiency and iron excess damage mitochondria and mitochondrial DNA in rats

Cocho, Speciation of iron in breast milk and infant formulas whey by size exclusion chromatography-high performance liquid chromatography and electrothermal atomic absorption spectrometry, Talanta 50 (2000) 1211-1222.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of some metal ions in various meat and baby food samples by atomic spectrometry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Dasbasi, S. Sacmaci, A. Ulgen, S. Kartal, Determination of some metal ions in various meat and baby food samples by atomic spectrometry, Food Chemistry 197 (2016) 107-113.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: More sensitive way to determine iron using an iron(II)-l,10-phenanthroline complex and capillary electrophoresis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Xu, P. Che, Y. F. Ma, More sensitive way to determine iron using an iron(II)-l,10-phenanthroline complex and capillary electrophoresis, J. Chromatogr. A 749 (1996) 287-294.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of iron(II) and total iron in environmental water samples by flow injection analysis with column preconcentration of chelating resin functionalized with N-hydroxyethylethylenediamine ligands and chemiluminescence detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Hirata, H. Yoshihara, M. Aihara, Determination of iron(II) and total iron in environmental water samples by flow injection analysis with column preconcentration of chelating resin functionalized with N-hydroxyethylethylenediamine ligands and chemiluminescence detection, Talanta 49 (1999) 1059-1067.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quinoline driven fluorescence turn on 1,3-Bis-calix[4]arene conjugate-based receptor to discriminate Fe3+ from Fe2+

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. K. Pathak, J. Dessingou, V. K. Hinge, A. G. Thawari, S. K. Basu, C. P. Rao, Quinoline driven fluorescence turn on 1,3-Bis-calix[4]arene conjugate-based receptor to discriminate Fe3+ from Fe2+, Anal. Chem. 85 (2013) 3707-3714.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel molecular tools to discriminate Fe3+ and Fe2+ by fluorescence via “turn-on” responses within neuronal cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. P. Murale, A. P. Singh, J. Lavoie, H. Liew, J. Cho, H. Lee, Y. Suh, D. G. Churchill, Novel molecular tools to discriminate Fe3+ and Fe2+ by fluorescence via “turn-on” responses within neuronal cells, Sens. Actuators B Chem. 185 (2013) 755-761.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chiral core-shell upconversion nanoparticle@MOF nanoassemblies for quantification and bioimaging of reactive oxygen species in vivo

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. L. Hao, X. L. Wu, M. Z. Sun, H. Y. Zhang, A. M. Yuan, L. G. Xu, C. L. Xu, H. Kuang, Chiral core-shell upconversion nanoparticle@MOF nanoassemblies for quantification and bioimaging of reactive oxygen species in vivo, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 141 (2019) 19373-19378.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Fan, L. Liu, F. Zhang, Exploiting lanthanide-doped upconversion nanoparticles with core/shell structures, Nano Today 25 (2019) 68-84.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Deep photoacoustic/luminescence/magnetic resonance multimodal imaging in living subjects using high-efficiency upconversion nanocomposites
